source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure to Create a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure to Create a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)
input_row.description: Setting up a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) protects the private network that has been established from any external networks from interfering. It allows internal networks to communicate with one another with the network that has been established.
input_row.rationale: Impact: Builds a strong connection between internal networks and the internet, and it secures your data from getting into the hand of an unauthorized party.
input_row.audit: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console • Sign in to the AWS Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ with your AWS account credentials. 2. Open the Amazon VPC Console • Navigate to the service using the Find Services search bar or by directly accessing the console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/. 3. Create a VPC • In the Amazon VPC console, click Your VPCs in the left-side menu. • Click on Create VPC to begin creating a new VPC. • Provide a name and the desired IPv4 CIDR block for your VPC. • Configure additional settings, such as IPv6 CIDR block, tenancy, and DNS resolution. • Click Create to create the VPC. 4. Create Subnets • In the Amazon VPC console, click Subnets in the left-side menu. • Click on Create subnet to create a subnet within the VPC. • Select the VPC you created in the previous step. • Provide a name, choose an availability zone, and specify the IPv4 CIDR block for the subnet. • Configure additional settings, such as IPv6 CIDR block and availability zone.  • Click Create to create the subnet. 5. Configure Route Tables • In the Amazon VPC console, click on Route Tables in the left-side menu. • Click on Create route table to create a new route table. • Provide a name for the route table and select the VPC you created earlier. • Click Create to create the route table. • Associate the route table with the desired subnets by selecting the route table and clicking on the Subnet associations tab. • Click Edit subnet associations and select the desired subnets to associate them with the route table. 6. Configure Security Groups • In the Amazon VPC console, click Security Groups in the left-side menu. • Click on Create security group to create a new security group. • Provide a name and description for the security group. • Select the VPC you created earlier. • Configure inbound and outbound rules to control network traffic to and from your RDS instances. • Click Create to create the security group. 7. Configure Network Access Control Lists (ACLs) • In the Amazon VPC console, click on Network ACLs in the left-side menu. • Click on Create network ACL to create a new network ACL. • Provide a name for the network ACL and select the VPC you created earlier. • Configure inbound and outbound rules to allow or deny specific types of traffic. • Associate the network ACL with the desired subnets by selecting the network ACL and clicking on the Subnet associations tab. • Click Edit subnet associations and select the desired subnets to associate them with the network ACL. 8. Use the VPC with Amazon RDS • Select the appropriate VPC, subnets, and security groups when creating an RDS instance. • Configure the database instance with the desired network and security settings within the chosen VPC.
input_row.impact: Builds a strong connection between internal networks and the internet, and it secures your data from getting into the hand of an unauthorized party.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

- **Automated:** The control pertains to the creation and configuration of a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and its associated components (subnets, route tables, security groups, and network ACLs) which can be verified via AWS APIs. These elements are standard AWS service properties, and the required configurations can be checked through various AWS services' APIs, making the control fully automatable.
  
- **Rationale for Automated Approach:**
  - AWS provides APIs to list and describe VPCs, subnets, route tables, security groups, and network ACLs. 
  - The `boto3` SDK has methods to interact with these resources such as `describe_vpcs`, `describe_subnets`, `describe_route_tables`, `describe_security_groups`, and `describe_network_acls`.
  - The AWS CLI also supports commands like `aws ec2 describe-vpcs`, `aws ec2 describe-subnets`, etc., to retrieve configuration data.
  - The control description involves configurations that are purely infrastructural and networking-based, which are typically exposed via AWS management APIs.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_vpc_configuration_compliance_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **AWS API calls or boto3 SDK methods to use:**
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2').describe_vpcs()` to list all VPCs and validate their configurations.
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2').describe_subnets()` to check subnets within the VPC.
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2').describe_route_tables()` to ensure route tables are correctly configured and associated with appropriate subnets.
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` to verify security group configurations within the VPC.
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2').describe_network_acls()` to examine the network ACLs associated with the VPC.

- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  - Enumerate all VPCs across accounts and regions.
  - Enumerate related subnets, route tables, security groups, and network ACLs for each VPC.

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Verify that each VPC has a valid CIDR block.
  - Check that subnets are configured with their CIDR blocks and associated with VPCs.
  - Validate that route tables are correctly configured and associated with subnets.
  - Confirm that security groups have been created with appropriate rules for RDS instances.
  - Ensure that network ACLs have been configured with necessary inbound and outbound rules.

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - Pass if the VPC, subnets, route tables, security groups, and network ACLs meet the desired configurations.
  - Fail if any component is missing or does not comply with the expected settings (e.g., missing subnets, incorrect route table associations, etc.).

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification as Automated is correct. The creation and configuration of a VPC and its components (subnets, route tables, security groups, and network ACLs) can indeed be verified through AWS APIs, boto3, and AWS CLI. These resources are well-documented and accessible via AWS management APIs, making automation feasible.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details are technically accurate. The boto3 methods and AWS CLI commands specified are valid and correctly used to describe and validate VPC configurations.
- The program name `aws_vpc_configuration_compliance_check` is descriptive but could be more specific to the security context, such as `aws_vpc_security_configuration_check`.
- The description of the exact configuration properties/settings to validate is comprehensive and aligns with best practices for VPC security.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming the program to `aws_vpc_security_configuration_check` to better reflect the focus on security compliance.
- **Additional Considerations:** 
  - Include checks for VPC Flow Logs to ensure they are enabled for monitoring traffic.
  - Validate that the VPC is not using default settings, which could pose security risks.
  - Consider using AWS Config rules to continuously monitor the compliance of VPC configurations.
  - Ensure that the VPC peering connections, if any, are also evaluated for security configurations.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment demonstrates a strong understanding of AWS capabilities and security best practices. The use of AWS APIs and automation tools is well-justified and aligns with industry standards.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Update the program name to `aws_vpc_security_configuration_check` for clarity and specificity regarding the security aspect of the compliance check.
- Consider adding additional checks for VPC Flow Logs and non-default settings to enhance the robustness of the compliance assessment.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that the existence and configuration of a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) can be fully verified using AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI, AWS Config). All relevant VPC properties (existence, CIDR block, subnets, route tables, security groups, network ACLs, and even VPC Flow Logs) are accessible via these APIs, making automation not only feasible but best practice for scalable compliance checks.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_ec2_vpc_existence_and_security_configuration_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: EC2 VPCs (Virtual Private Clouds)
- API/CLI Method: 
  - boto3: ec2.describe_vpcs(), ec2.describe_subnets(), ec2.describe_route_tables(), ec2.describe_security_groups(), ec2.describe_network_acls(), ec2.describe_flow_logs()
  - AWS CLI: aws ec2 describe-vpcs, aws ec2 describe-subnets, aws ec2 describe-route-tables, aws ec2 describe-security-groups, aws ec2 describe-network-acls, aws ec2 describe-flow-logs
- Properties to Check:
  - At least one non-default VPC exists.
  - VPC has a valid, non-overlapping IPv4 CIDR block.
  - Subnets, route tables, security groups, and network ACLs are configured (not left at insecure defaults).
  - VPC Flow Logs are enabled for monitoring.
- Pass Criteria:
  - At least one VPC exists (not just the default VPC).
  - VPC has a valid CIDR block and is not using insecure defaults.
  - Associated subnets, route tables, security groups, and network ACLs are present and not open to 0.0.0.0/0 without justification.
  - VPC Flow Logs are enabled.
- Fail Criteria:
  - No VPCs exist (other than the default).
  - VPCs have insecure or default configurations.
  - No VPC Flow Logs enabled.
  - Security groups or network ACLs are overly permissive.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation. The only minor difference was the program name; I selected a name that is both specific and descriptive per the requirements.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
